transcript: Scientists are discovering that when you touch someone you communicate very specific emotions such as sympathy discussed gratitude or even love the current issue of the scientific journal emotion features a series of studies about touch reporter Michelle Trudeau touch base with the lead researcher psychologist map curtain Stein from DePaul University in Greencastle Indiana decided to study touch while he was watching parents interacting with their babies making faces and cooling sounds squeezing stroking nuzzling them and all of a sudden it struck me one day and I thought you know I wonder if touch can communicate distinct emotions much like the face in the voice decades of research has been done on the face and the voice and the distinct emotions that they communicate but type should been relatively neglected by researchers until Hartenstein stepped in and began his experiments we invited to participants into the lab and we put a curtain up between those 2 people so they couldn't see or hear each other one participant the sender was told to try and communicate 12 different emotions one by one to the other participant the receiver receiver would put his or her arm underneath the curtain on to the sender sign the sender within touch the receivers form trying to communicate the specific emotion such as envy fear love and Paris mint anger gratitude pride discussed the receiver had to then decide which a motion was being communicated.

About 20 years ago Kent Anger and Barry Johnson came up with 750 chemicals that could harm the brain during development. Nobody has since then dared to update that number, it’s just a guess today, there has to be more than a thousand if there was 750 twenty years ago. But the problem is also that we have put too little emphasis in this type of, uh, research.

For example, it has taken so far the OECD 10 years to devise a battery of tests that they could recommend for systematic testing of chemicals for developmental neurotoxicity. That panel, that battery, has not yet been completed and authorized by OECD so it’s taking way, way, way too long because it is complicated. But there is so much at stake.

Children are just losing IQ points and losing their concentration span, memory or motor functions. But in the present world where there’s so much emphasis on knowledge and brain functions this can also translate into dollars. The EPA has calculated that every time a child loses one IQ point because of chemical pollution it costs society something like $8,000 or $10,000.

I understand that one major concern of yours in running a successful company, especially in the current economic climate, is the question of worker loyalty. Are you saying that such a thing no longer exists and, if so, what are some of the reasons for this, and how important is loyalty to the smooth and successful running of a company such as yours? Well, I do think it's important, but I also realize that we can't go back to the old-fashioned sense of loyalty where an employee would spend his whole working life with one company or business. Our grandparents', even our parents', generation expected long-term employment, and their loyalty was rewarded with health care and a pension. This is no longer the case. Many companies are no longer willing, or perhaps even able, to provide such a financial package. Besides, to a younger generation, sticking at the same job all your life isn't a very exciting or inspiring prospect. Some reasons for this might be the shortening of contracts, outsourcing, automation, and people holding down more than one job. In other words, we've all had to adapt to the realities of a rapidly changing, fast-paced economy. However, all this is not to say that loyalty is dead, rather it has changed emphasis. Today, it is more about trust, in his work for as long as she or he is there. People change jobs a lot more these days, but I still believe that a company is better off with at least a core of people who stay for the long term.

I'd now like to turn to the Roman, um, Latin writers of the period. Their achievements, painting and sculpture - were rather second - rate, and mostly in imitation of the Greeks. And, it's true that Latin writers also wanted to emulate the Greeks and used their writers as models, but somehow the language wouldn't allow it. What I mean to say is, the particular qualities of the Latin tongue made for the excellence of their writings, especially the poetry. For one thing, Latin is capable of great compression - that is to say, it can convey in five or six words what we would need twice the number to say. What also helped Latin writing flourish was the nature of Roman education. Whatever its faults as they would appear to us, its linguistic and literary emphasis seemed designed to produce orators and writers. A drawback of the system was that education was only available to the richer classes of society, and so Roman literature is mainly a product of those classes. This in turn meant that the subject matter was fairly limited and narrow in its social reach - though there were exceptions.

Some economists argue that financial aid to developing countries is, in the long run, inefficient and even counter-productive. However, there are two main types of aid that we will deal with here. First, there is long-term aid to countries such as Ethiopia and Somalia, where there are recurrent problems such as drought and poor agricultural production, and where there is little or no industry to speak of - problems that won't go away with an injection of money. Then there is emergency aid, which also appeals for contributions from the public, when a disaster of one kind or another strikes. Recent examples would be the earthquake in Haiti, and the tsunami in Japan. In the case of emergency aid, it arrives in the first instance as food, clothing, shelter, and medical aid, all of which are of immediate practical use and great benefit. With countries that have long-term developmental problems, just pumping in money is not enough and, sad to say, a lot of the money doesn't go where it should. What is more important is providing know-how: teaching the skills and expertise needed to help develop the economy and social services such as health, sanitation, and so on. It is also necessary to help build the institutions - a bureaucracy, if you like - that can organize and run these services.

Interviewer - Now, professor, recently you wrote a letter to a leading national newspaper complaining about falling standards in both written and spoken language among students, even graduates, and saying that you deplore the way English is being debased by change and dumbing down. Interviewee - Yes, I said that standards are falling and that very few graduates these days can write a comprehensible essay. Their grammar and syntax is all over the place and I do have certain regrets over the way some words have now become unusable in their full meaning because they've been sloppily misused by those who should know better, such as journalists. So, because they use, say, 'enormity' to mean something very big instead of something very wicked, I can no longer use the word in its correct sense without being misunderstood. And there are hundreds of other erases like this. But, of course, language changes, and meanings shift and change emphasis, and it's as useless to complain about that as it is to moan about the weather. The point I was making was that, at the earliest possible level, children should be made familiar with the basics of grammar and syntax, how to put sentences together, and so on. But I'm not suggesting going back to the days when, as I did, you had to analyze sentences in minute detail as if you were doing Latin. Though, of course, there is something to be said for having that kind of detailed understanding of the language.

Perhaps the first example of what could be called a newspaper was the Acta Diurna - roughly, "Daily News" - that Julius Caesar introduced in 59 BC. This was a handwritten news-sheet posted daily in the Forum at Rome and in other common meeting places around the city. Of course, a lot of the news would be out of date in the sense that, for example, it took a long time for reports of a victory in a distant country to get back to Rome. Nonetheless, a lot of the items included are similar to those found in more modern newspapers: news of battles, as already mentioned, as well as political and military appointments, political events, and even a social diary recording marriages, births, and deaths. One mustn't forget sport - if that is what you'd call it. Just like modern fans of football, sports-minded Romans could keep up with the latest results of the gladiator contests. People who lived in the provinces and wanted to be kept up to date would send scribes to Rome to copy the news and have them send it back by letter. Many of these scribes could make extra money by providing the news to more than one client. Quite a few of them were slaves and would go on to use the extra money earned to buy their freedom.

As you have probably noticed, fashions and tastes change quite noticeably over the years, most obviously perhaps in clothes, hairstyles and popular music. The reputations of writers and artists are no different, though the alternating periods of being in and then out of fashion are longer. A poet may no longer appeal to a large reading public for a number of reasons, though we must keep in mind that poetry is a minority taste and its readership is relatively small in the first place. So, um, I want to look at the reputation of Alexander Pope. In his own lifetime, he was praised and idolized by both his friends and the literary world in general, though he did have some rather vicious enemies. Yet both friends and enemies seemed more concerned with either praising or attacking his character and morals than with properly assessing the poems themselves. The Romantics, of course, had little time for him. Indeed, the Romantic movement, in poetry at least, was an attempt to make a complete break with the strict formality and rationality of the Augustan poets, of whom Pope was the most notable example. They did not regard him as a real poet and complained of his ignorance of nature in the sense of mountains, trees, and flowers. The Victorians were even less responsive to Pope and this was probably the period when his reputation was at its lowest. In fact, it wasn't until the 1920s that Pope and the Augustan poets were re-evaluated, and given the attention and respect they deserved.

Interviewer: Professor, could you say a bit more about this psycho- geography you mentioned earlier?

Interviewee: Well, I could give you a short history of the subject, though, um, it's not a subject in the academic sense as far as I'm aware - or at least not at the college where I teach.

Interviewer: But what is it exactly? It sounds rather complicated, a mixture of, what, psychology and geography?

Interviewee: No, not at all. Well, the first theorist of it was a Frenchman, Guy Debord, and basically, in his words, it's the study of the effects of the geographical environment on the emotions and behavior of people. Put like that, of course, it doesn't sound like anything new -people have always been aware at some level of the effect of their surroundings on their emotions and way of life. And this includes not just buildings, but the landscape as a whole, the climate, and so on. But it is mainly an urban thing, and these days people who call themselves psycho-geographers tend to be harsh critics of the way modern city planners develop urban space. Really, I suppose, it asks people to pay more attention to their environment, be critical of it, and to note not just the aspects we've already mentioned but the atmosphere created by them as well. I could go on and tell you how to go about it, but all you have to do is go online and you'll find a site for almost any city you care to name. It's worth looking into.

It is almost impossible to talk about the history of the novel without starting with a definition of it, which is by no means easy to do. We all know what we ourselves mean by a novel and have our favorite novelists, whether they are from the heyday of Victorian fiction - Dickens, say, or George Eliot - or someone more modern or postmodern, for example, B.S. Johnson, who is famed for writing a novel that was bought in a box, with loose pages that you could read in any order. Again, you might be a fan of crime or detective fiction, which brings the added complication of genre. That is, does the fact that a work of fiction is comedy, tragedy, satire, ghost story, and so on, affect our definition? Anyway, as far as the history of the English novel is concerned, we're on fairly solid ground when we date the first novels to the late 17th and early 18th centuries. The first that is still read as a novel in the way we read novels now is Bunyan's Pilgrim's Progress, which did have an enormous effect on English prose writing, but for me it would be Defoe's Robinson Crusoe. Now, people have tried to locate the beginnings even earlier than this, to Elizabethan prose writing and even further back, but I think this is to lose sight of what a novel is and does and confuses any kind of fiction with the true novel.

It was the philosopher Thomas Nagel, I think, who came up with the thought experiment of trying to imagine what it's like to be a bat - though I suppose any animal would do. The point is, of course, that you can't, just as you can't know what it's really like to be another person - I mean know rather than imagine. How much can we know of what goes on in another person's mind? We observe people, watch what they do, listen to what they say - and other sounds they may make - see how they respond to their environment, their likes and dislikes, what they eat, and so on. And from this we have learned to make fairly accurate predictions about how they'll behave in certain situations, yet we still can't be sure what thoughts have passed through their minds and certainly not what it feels like to them. We can guess, based on personal experience in similar situations, so we must assume that humans are alike enough for us to be reasonably certain that, for example, we experience the color red in roughly the same way, or in the same ways. But, again, to you red might bring to mind thoughts of blood and death, whereas to someone else warmth or excitement. And the differences can't just be cultural.

One of the problems with primary teaching is that you are expected to be a jack-of-all-trades. As you are aware, this is quite unrealistic. Very few people are equally good at Music and English and Science and Art and Maths and P.E. Increasingly specialist teachers are being employed in primary schools. The argument against moving completely in this direction is the pastoral needs of younger students. The relationship a student develops with a single teacher can be valuable. The routine of moving from room to room  or indeed of teaching style can be problematic for younger students. The ideal situation is a school where teacher’s special interests and passions can be complemented by the skill of their colleagues. Partial use of special teachers can be beneficial. As a teacher, if you love teaching Maths, then teach it! If Art is your thing, go for it! Children will be motivated by teachers who are passionate about what they are doing. This doesn’t negate the need for primary teachers to be solid in a range of areas but it does try to acknowledge the simple fact that no one is good at.

Most students at some point of their high school education will be faced with a choice of studying history or geography. For my part, I firmly believe they should both be compulsory, given this is often not realistic how do we best guide students in their choice. The guiding tenant should be what the student is interested in. Students natural laning seems so often these days to be overlooked. For goodness sake let them do what I want to do not what they think will lead into future economic success or what their parents think they should study. In short talk to them about their strengths and interests. History will clearly involve more essay writing which may be limiting because some students, geography on the other hand calls the practical skills field work and a different style of writing. Encourage students to talk to all the students about their experiences and if I were happy with the choices I made. Looking at the top of assessment tasks given for the various courses could also provide the on certain student with a little more idea about what to expect.

My research was conducted to consider teacher attitudes towards the implementation of national testing the students in literacy and numeracy in years 3, 5, 7 and 9. The research was qualitative in nature and involved interviewing classroom teachers in the field post during the first and second administration of national testing. One of the objectives of the research was to see if there was a nexus between teacher attitudes and students performance scores. The research found that students scores were correlated with teacher attitudes. There was a greater link between students prior achievements, school economic resources and students socio economic backgrounds, with the final of these having the most sizeable impact on student performance. I anticipate that the research team will reconduct this work again during the next testing cycle to confirm these findings.

For the past few decades, mushrooms have gained much popularity as the source of making different items of food. A lot of research is going on in developing different ways to make food products from mushrooms. Many of us may not have the idea about the medicinal value of mushrooms. As a matter of fact mushrooms are a rich source of minerals, vitamin D, firemen, riboflavin and niacin. Beside this, mushrooms are also rich in dietary fiber and almost all amino acids. What is more important to notice about mushrooms, is the fact that despite being rich in all these essential nutrients the low in fat and calories. There are about 100,000 species of mushrooms available in the world. Surprisingly only 700 out of these are used for food. About 200 species of mushrooms are believed to possess medicinal value. Others or more may also be added to this list in the future. Extensive research that scientists have been carrying out on mushroom as fruit. A mushroom is a kind of adaptogen like many botanical species found in the world, adaptogens also called biological response modifiers. These help the body in order to attempt to various types of stress that maybe environmental or psychological. Adaptogens function in such a manner that they benefit almost all crucial systems of the human body. For example, the nervous system, endocrine and adrenal. These also boost the capacity of the immune system in order that it works efficiently.

The Great Depression began in 1929. It was a great recession, rather a slump in the economies of North America, Europe and other industrialized areas of the world. The Great Depression lasted for a decade i.e. tell 1939. The Great Depression took place with the collapse of the stock market prices on the New York Stock Exchange in October 19, 1929. This put a severe strain on the banks and other financial institutions. Consequently, many banks were forced into insolvency. By 1933 out of about 25,000 banks in America, 11,000 had failed. This led to a drastic reduction in spending and hence demand went down. This further aggravated the situation. The outputs in factories fell down and there was a sharp increase in unemployment. By 1932, the total output had reduced by about 55 percent, while unemployment had shot up by 29 percent. The Great Depression had started in America but slowly began to spread to other parts of the world too. European economies with which America had close trade relations. Almost all countries in an attempt to protect their domestic production impose tariffs increasing the existing ones. They also set quotas on foreign imports. By 1932, the total value of international trade had fallen by almost half.

Natural diamonds are made from the element carbon deep within the earth's crust. Some are created at depths of about 180km below the earth's surface, while others are made at depths of 300-400km. The rarest diamonds are pink and others are blue, amber, emerald or violet. Natural diamonds are the world's hardest known substance. It is estimated that the world's known supplies of diamonds will be depleted within 30 to 50 years. About 80% of all mined diamonds are used for industrial applications like drilling, milling, and sawing. The rest are used for jewelry or investing. Less than 2% of the diamonds mined are of such fine quality they can be considered investment quality.

Buying a new, pre-owned or used car from a franchise dealer is a good choice for anyone looking for the best deals on certain makes. Only certified dealers can give you the best solution. This is because the manufacturer has approved the dealership and certified the service technicians to maintain their standards. Even if you are purchasing a certified pre-owned vehicle from a franchise dealer, you can rest assured it has gone through a rigorous inspection process according to the manufacturer's standards to make sure the vehicle is in prime working order. They will also help you to evaluate the exact resale value of the car at the time period you wish to sell.

Saturated fats are the unhealthy fats associated with a myriad of health problems. They contain virtually nothing nutritious or good for your body – their only role is they can be burned as energy. You don't need saturated fats at all, but the typical diet is jam-packed with them. For health reasons, you want to consume as little saturated fats as possible. Saturated fats are generally solid at room temperature and can be found in butter, margarine, dairy, cheese and meats. I am not recommending you to become a vegetarian and eliminate meat from your diet, but make sure when you are at the meat counter you select lean cuts.

It's now possible to get a great job in computers that isn't about science, maths programming or accountancy. Increasingly, humanity-based skills are often perceived as more valuable and computers can get you into a whole new range of professions evolving around games production, multimedia, digital video or publishing on the internet. Within these and even more traditional professions, such as journalism, film, television, publishing, advertising, design and music, computers and digital technology have become widespread. In the future there will probably be very few jobs or professions that won't require some degree of computer literacy.

Profit or net income before taxes are what every business should be striving for, whether it's a small internet based company or a multi-billion revenue corporation. And while sales are relative to the size of budget, profit is the true measure of success. Even internet IPO success stories like Amazon will be short lived if profitability is not achieved before investors' patience becomes critical. The stock price can go just so high if there is not the means to pay dividends. Eventually, the treasure chest that is being robbed to pay Peter will become empty. The bottom-line must be black more than red for any business to remain solvent.

As competition continues to drive down ticket prices, airlines struggle to trim fat. More fat means more fuel and airlines are investigating how to lighten their planes in an effort to reduce fuel costs. Two bag limits and excess baggage fees are the norm for most airlines, while some airlines have taken away free meals and replaced them with overpriced snacks. Furthermore, some airlines say that labor pay cuts are necessary to survive, but the pilot's unions are holding their ground. It's hard to imagine what will happen next, but it's sure to have an impact on travelers' wallets.

One in 2 Americans will need a lawyer's advice in the next twelve months. Most of these people will try to handle it on their own, because they think they can't afford a lawyer. But, if you needed an operation would you do it yourself because you couldn't afford a surgeon? Of course not. You're not qualified and you know it. That's why people are so desperate to find affordable health insurance. The law is just as complex as surgery, and just as dangerous. When you go into court, it isn't about right and wrong, it's about winning and your opponent knows it.

Many people think hypnosis is some other dimension of consciousness. It's actually a very normal, natural awareness that we're moving in and out of all the time. When you're driving down the road on autopilot, and your mind drifts off, and all of a sudden you're aware that you've driven past your turnoff. Or when you're at the movie theater, and get so involved with the action on the screen that you're barely aware of the rest of the people in the audience. These are all examples of the state of mind that we call hypnosis.

The main idea behind networking is to gain the respect of other businesses and individuals for the contribution you and your company can make. You rarely gain any alliances from an opt-in list that is made up of different businesses or individuals trying to push their products or services onto each other. It's nothing but a nameless, faceless barrage of ads. And, for all the virtual legwork required in news groups, the very few connections you might make are outweighed by the time you will spend doing it. With the different types of electronic meetings that are available today, they are comparably better than newsgroups or opt-in's, but still cannot compare to real networking.

The rise of computer use and flat, light-touch keyboards that permit high speed typing have resulted in an epidemic of injuries of the hands, arms, and shoulders. Use of pointing devices like mice and trackballs are as much a cause, if not more so. The thousands of repeated keystrokes and long periods of clutching and dragging with mice slowly accumulates damage to the body. Another name  for the condition is Cumulative Trauma Disorder. This can happen even more quickly as a result of typing technique and body positions that place unnecessary stress on the tendons and nerves in the hand, wrist, arms, and even the shoulders and neck.

In time I've learned if a camera fits well into my hand it will tend to work well for me. This might sound odd but think about it this way: If you're handling a camera that feels awkward it's going to reflect in your results. I had a Minolta Maxxum 7000 for many years. It was a big, clunky unit but it was a good 'fit' for me and it produced great shots. My rule of thumb therefore is if it's a snug fit and the main controls are convenient for your fingers and it's within your budget, you can be pretty sure it will do the job for you.

Nowadays, cosmetic surgery is at the top of the list of remedies for saggy, wrinkled skin. And it's not just women undergoing these procedures. According to the Association of Plastic Surgeons, in 2017 American men accounted for 1.75 million cosmetic procedures, almost as many as women. The primary procedures for men are nose reshaping, liposuction and hair transplantation, while for women it's breast augmentation, eyelid surgery and chemical peels. Non-surgical procedures like Botox and laser hair removal are also on the increase. Most cosmetic surgery is not covered by health insurance though, unless the procedure is health related, so if cost is an issue you should investigate the various non-surgical or less invasive treatments available.

As a Traditional Chinese Medicine (TCM) practitioner I am often asked about the effects of stress on health. According to TCM, stress mainly affects the liver.  However, depending on each individual it may also affect the digestive organs, the bowels and/or the heart. When stress affects digestion it can cause such problems as poor appetite, bloating, belching and nausea. If the bowels are affected it causes diarrhea and/or constipation and maybe conditions like irritable bowel syndrome. When the heart is affected by stress it causes anxiety and palpitations. If the liver is affected one might feel irritable or frustrated or moody or have pain or discomfort in the region of the liver.

Inventors can obtain one of 3 kinds of patents to protect their new invention. Usually, they will hire a lawyer to aid them in the process of securing their patent. Some attorneys specialize in patent law, meaning they know the various ins and outs of the business. If you're an inventor and you're hiring a lawyer to help you in this process expect to pay a substantial fee because of the specialty of their service. Not seeking legal advice and assistance in this area is risky business. Finally, be certain there is a market for your invention before you go through the whole process only to find out nobody has any interest in or need for what you've invented.

Archery has a rich history of over 8000 years of use in hunting, warfare and survival. Nowadays it is more of a popular sport or pastime. Initially, bows and arrows were used for hunting and survival and later as a standard weapon in warfare by the Greeks, the Chinese, Native Americans and other cultures. Bows and arrows as weapons began to be replaced in medieval times by swords, then later on by guns. Although crossbows were developed, which had more aim and control than bows and arrows, swords were a lot sturdier and then of course guns had far more power and as weapons offered more devastating results in battle.

The best place to start looking into your family history is your own family. Stories passed down through the generations will help you begin your search. So, talk to your family members and relatives to see what they know about your family's history. The elderly can have in-depth knowledge of your family tree for generations. Typically, they know such things as what your ancestors did for a living, where they lived and when and how they died. However, be aware that family recollections especially of the elderly can be inaccurate or wrong. Try to verify dates and events by obtaining copies of birth, death and marriage certificates. Other valuable documents are things like family photographs, personal diaries and letters.

Any advertising a company does will be ineffective if it's not supported by great sales technique and outstanding customer service. An ad that calls to attention a line of goods will not sell those goods if the company fails to back it up with a well directed in-store sales effort. The purpose of advertising then is to entice people, to get them to come into the store and once in it's the job of the salespeople to convert them to buyers with great technique and service. If a high level of service can't be consistently offered it's pointless advertising and eventually the company may even be out of business.

People who study astrology generally fall into one of two groups. There are those who genuinely try to divulge the secrets of the universe and the heavenly bodies and understand their unique relationships, and then transfer their insight to events on earth. The second group includes con artists trying to make money from the naive and those eager to throw money at practitioners pretending to understand the universe. These tricksters then proceed to tell their clients what they want to hear, for a sizable fee of course. Their next step is telling people what they can do to avoid tragedy in their life or to improve their chances for success.

People are becoming increasingly concerned about the negative impact on health of chlorine in water supplies and swimming in chlorinated pool water. Have you ever noticed how dry your skin is after you swim in a chlorinated pool? Or that it's red or blotchy? And what about what it does to your hair? Chlorine is added to pool water to attack living organic matter, but we are living organic matter too! You should replace your swimming pool's existing filter system with an alternative non-chlorine system. This way you can stop the harmful effects of chlorine and still stop contaminants that find their way into your swimming pool water.

The first but definitely not the easiest thought that comes to most people when they are neck-deep in debt is to file for bankruptcy. Filing for bankruptcy seems to be the last straw left in the deluge of unpaid bills and abusive creditor calls. The situation is somewhat like this: You buy whatever catches your fancy and you're thankful you have a credit card. It feels good as long as you are spending, but when it's pay time, you realize your misdoing. Abusive creditor calls begin, you have trouble sleeping, so what do you do? File for bankruptcy. Sadly, more and more people are doing this today.

There are two types of solar energy, thermal and electric. Thermal energy is all around us. It heats the earth, our bodies and our homes and it's free. Thermal energy is also used to heat water for domestic use. Electric energy uses the power of the sun to produce electricity through solar cells, otherwise known as photovoltaics. The advantages of solar energy are it is cost effective and it's environmentally friendly. Likewise the maintenance of solar energy systems is relatively low. The downside is the initial set up costs can be high and no energy is produced at night time. On cloudy days the production of solar energy may also be affected.

Camping is a delightfully adventurous way to spend a break with mates or family. Camping basically means living in a tent pitched on flat, bare ground. The biggest plus is you can have a good vacation without breaking the bank. Unlike staying in a hotel, camping is not associated with hassles like deciding what type and class of room you need and whether you can afford it. Camping also takes you away from all the hurly burly of the big, flashy hotels and lends you the opportunity to follow your own style. With camping just decide what kind of grounds you want to go for and your preference for activities and amenities.

Biology is a fascinating subject to study. It is full of learning and it is also exciting since it involves the colorful world of animals and plants, which to most people are very interesting to learn about. Biological studies for students used to be primarily through books, but now they can learn also through online videos. Online videos really are an effective media for learning and education, in all fields, but especially when it comes to the natural world. With books students often have to imagine how certain animals or plants look. Books may be illustrated, but videos are in motion making it far more true-to-life in the eyes of the students.

Every business has competition, that is unless you have a completely unique product or service. To rise above your competitors you have to make sure you're the key company, the one your customers turn to above all others. If you're newer or smaller than them, though, how do you do this? Be more flexible with your terms and conditions than your competitors and offer more personalized service and attention. Don't take more than 24 hours to respond to queries and be willing to help customers sometimes without always expecting something in return. You can't always be looking at the dollar signs!

When it comes to truck tyres various manufacturers offer a family of tyres often called trail tyres. Typically, these are tyres designed for light trucks or sport utility vehicles and which are mostly used on the street. They're quieter, they get better petrol mileage and they last longer than off-road tyres. The tread patterns are designed to provide greater comfort or performance on the street, although this can compromise their off-road capability. When choosing tyres the tread pattern is very important to consider and more importantly so if you are taking your truck on an off-road adventure.

Last year in the UK over 4.6 million people had at least one trip for occasions such as a honeymoon or an anniversary. Bed and Breakfasts have responded to this market in various ways. Many B&Bs are in attractive, older-style homes. They exude charm as they're usually furnished with antiques and are set in picturesque countryside areas. You can luxuriate lazily in fine linen on a comfortable bed, soak in a relaxing bubble bath or snuggle up in armchairs to enjoy a  roaring hot fireplace. B&B hospitality is typically warm and personable because for the innkeepers it's not just a job - their livelihood depends on what they offer, so you will rarely be disappointed.

Many people don't know that their general health is directly related to the health of their gums. In fact there is a correlation between gum disease and diabetes, heart attack, stroke and lung disease. Studies also show that the presence of periodontal diseases actually affects longevity. Research by Emory University and the Centers for Disease Control discovered that the mortality rate is far higher, by over 40% , in people with gingivitis and periodontal disease compared to those who don't. Keeping your gums and teeth clean and healthy may help reduce your risk of many serious illnesses, so it's well worth your while to pay daily attention to your oral health.

A home-based business can be a great way to support yourself if you hate working for others. Don't chuck your regular job in just yet though. You might think it will be hard and take up too much of your free time doing both, but your home business won't be profitable in the beginning, so you will still need your regular income. Your home business can still fail though, and usually, it's people who didn't work hard enough, or people who chose an overly competitive business, or a business that has no market at all who fail. So before you leave that job or boss, you loath to start your own business, do lots and lots of research!

Goat farming is a profitable and enjoyable business activity growing in popularity, due to consumer demand for alternatives to cow products. Many cow farmers are switching to goat farming. Large goat farms may have more than 500 goats and like cows, goats are milked twice daily. They are milked by machine or by hand depending on the size of the farm and the kind of techniques, and workforce the goat farm has. If the goats are being bred to supply meat, the farmer generally considers their breed, then he decides the ideal weight they should reach as different breeds grow to different weights.

America is recognized as the land of opportunity, where one could accomplish anything they put their mind to, no matter who they are. Thousands of people immigrate to the United States every year from different parts of the world to have access to these kinds of opportunities.

Many people are social media fanatics. They are always looking at their mobile phones and updating their Twitter or Facebook accounts. They take photos of what they eat or of themselves, which is called 'selfies'. When people are not oversharing, they are continually looking at what friends and family members are sharing. Social media may become addicting.

Many young European boys and girls dream of becoming a rock star at one time or another in their lives. European culture is mostly defined by the music young people fancy. During the 1970s, music in the Europe Union was greatly influenced by the political issues of the day. Some call the decade a revolutionary period in the country's history, and music was a big part of that revolution.\n\nWhen John was young, he was in love with the Beatles. The English band was possibly the most impactful group of the generation.

The space shuttle program began in the early 80s and proceeded to this day. The squadron consists of various shuttles that are designed to go into space, establish an orbit, and do a variety of tasks. It was heralded as a new era in space travel. In the past, space exploration vehicles were used once, and then discarded.

Little Billy wanted to join the game one night and asked his brothers if he could play. 'Please, Luca. Please let me play with you tonight," he asked. His older brother thought about it for a moment, and said, "Okay, Billy. Tonight you can play.' He didn't think he was going to be able to play the game, but to his surprise, he was not bad at all. The object of the game is to bankrupt the other players and have the most money at the end of the game. You buy properties with money collected by moving around the board. The more capital you have in your bank, the more property you can buy.

The third Monday in January is an American federal holiday, Martin Luther King Jr. Day. It falls near the birthday of the civil rights leader, who was assassinated in 1968. King, a minister, became known for non-violently protesting the treatment of African-Americans in the United States and laws that discriminated against Blacks. Specifically, King opposed segregation that separated blacks and whites in public restrooms, public pools, public schools, on buses, and at restaurants. King led many marches in the United States, especially in the South where segregation was especially practiced and enforced, and in Washington D.C. His movement on Washington D.C. was perhaps his most famous one, where he gave the 'I have a Dream' speech.

Space is the boundless three-dimensional extent in which objects and events have relative position and direction. Physical space is often conceived in three linear dimensions, although modern physicists usually consider it, with time, to be part of a boundless four-dimensional continuum known as spacetime. The concept of space is considered to be of fundamental importance to an understanding of the physical universe. However, disagreement continues between philosophers over whether it is itself an entity

Many American singers began their journey in someone's garage. One famous singer to start this way was Eros. Jennifer was convinced she could become the next star with a little chance and a lot of practice.\n\nShe practiced regularly over the years and soon became good enough to perform in front of some young audience, but she never hit the big time, unfortunately. The road to success in the music industry is not an easy one to travel, but that does not deter some American dreamers.

The Sun is composed primarily of the chemical elements hydrogen and helium. At this time in the Sun's life, they account for 74.9% and 23.8% of the mass of the Sun in the photosphere, respectively. All heavier elements, called metals in astronomy, account for less than 2% of the mass, with oxygen (roughly 1% of the Sun's mass), carbon (0.3%), neon (0.2%), and iron (0.2%) being the most abundant.

Most lizards are quadrupedal, running with a strong side-to-side motion. Others are legless, and have long snake-like bodies. Some such as the forest-dwelling Draco lizards are able to glide. They are often territorial, the males fighting off other males and signalling, often with brightly colours, to attract mates and to intimidate rivals.

Research shows that most people spend on average almost 4 hours a day on social networking sites. That's nearly 30 hours a week. While some people need and use social media for work or to stay in touch with friends, other people find that using social media so much causes anxiety and stress. People also tend to use social media as a way to procrastinate.

In many plants, a flower is its most colourful part. We say the plant 'flowers', 'is flowering' or 'is in flower' when this colourful part begins to grow bigger and open out. There are many different kinds of flowers in different areas in the world. Even in the coldest places, for example the Arctic, flowers can grow during a few months. Flowers may grow separately on the plant, or they may grow together in an inflorescence.
Astronomy is one of the oldest sciences. Ancient people used the positions of the stars to navigate and to find out when was the best time to plant crops. Astronomy is very similar to astrophysics.

Military science fiction stories happen during wars. These wars can be between different countries, different planets, or between different species. The stories are told by characters who are soldiers. They include detail about military technology, rules, and history. Some Military science fiction may be similar to real historical conflicts.
A smile is a face made by flexing the muscles near both ends of the mouth. The smile can also be made through the eyes. Smiles usually show happiness. A smile can be natural or fake.

The Sun is roughly middle-aged; it has not changed dramatically for more than four billion[a] years, and will remain fairly stable for more than another five billion years. It currently fuses about 600 million tons of hydrogen into helium every second, converting 4 million tons of matter into energy every second as a result. This energy, which can take between 10,000 and 170,000 years to escape from its core, is the source of the Sun's light and heat.

James couldn't believe what he heard. Soon, almost all employees gathered around the lunch room TV set watching in terror the events taking place. It was a dark day for the United States, but it was also the commencement of a new era in space travel. The country rebounded from this failure and restarted its program after a few years. There have been other accidents in the program, and most notably the Columbia, which deteriorated upon reentry in February 2003, but the U.S. will not be deterred. Traveling to Space is dangerous, but the spirit of the American people will not be contained.

Flowers are an important evolutionary advance made by flowering plants. Some flowers are dependent upon the wind to move pollen between flowers of the same species. Many others rely on insects or birds to move pollen. The role of flowers is to produce seeds, which are contained in fruits. Fruits and seeds are a means of dispersal. Plants do not move, but wind, animals, and birds spread the plants across the landscape.

Early astronomers used only their eyes to look at the stars. They made maps of the constellations and stars for religious reasons and calendars to work out the time of year. Early civilizations such as the Maya people and the Ancient Egyptians built simple observatories and drew maps of the stars positions. They also began to think about the place of Earth in the universe. For a long time, people thought Earth was the center of the universe, and that the planets, the stars, and the sun went around it. This is known as geocentrism.

The opening of this new window on the Universe saw the discovery of entirely new things, for example pulsars, which sent regular pulses of radio waves out into space. The waves were first thought to be alien in origin because the pulses were so regular that it implied an artificial source.

Violence in sports involves crossing the line between fair competition and intentional aggressive violence. Athletes, coaches, fans, and parents sometimes unleash violent behaviour on people or property, in misguided shows of loyalty, dominance, anger, or celebration. Rioting or hooliganism by fans in particular is a problem at some national and international sporting contests.

Since the 1990s, computer analysis has contributed significantly to chess theory, particularly in the endgame. The IBM computer Deep Blue was the first machine to overcome a reigning World Chess Champion in a match when it defeated Garry Kasparov in 1997. The rise of strong chess engines runnable on hand-held devices has led to increasing concerns about cheating during tournaments.

Extratropical cyclones begin as waves in large regions of enhanced mid-latitude temperature contrasts called baroclinic zones. These zones contract and form weather fronts as the cyclonic circulation closes and intensifies. Later in their life cycle, extratropical cyclones occlude as cold air masses undercut the warmer air and become cold core systems. A cyclone's track is guided over the course of its 2 to 6-day life cycle by the steering flow of the subtropical jet stream.

The Sun generates most space weather, which can affect power generation and transmission systems on Earth and interfere with, and even damage, satellites and space probes. Numerous spacecraft dedicated to observing the Sun, beginning with the Apollo Telescope Mount, have been launched and still others have had solar observation as a secondary objective.

In the past, those who were disabled were often not eligible for public education. Children with disabilities were repeatedly denied an education by physicians or special tutors. Early physicians set the foundation for special education today. They focused on individualized instruction and functional skills. In its early years, special education was only provided to people with severe disabilities, but more recently it has been opened to anyone who has experienced difficulty learning.

Subtropical cyclones began to receive names off the official tropical cyclone list in the Atlantic Basin in 2002. They have broad wind patterns with maximum sustained winds located farther from the center than typical tropical cyclones, and exist in areas of weak to moderate temperature gradient.

The competition element of sport, along with the aesthetic appeal of some sports, result in the popularity of people attending to watch sport being played. This has led to the specific phenomenon of spectator sport. Both amateur and professional sports attract spectators, both in person at the sport venue, and through broadcast media including radio, television and internet broadcast. Both attendance in person and viewing remotely can incur a sometimes substantial charge, such as an entrance ticket, or pay-per-view television broadcast.

Technology can be most broadly defined as the entities, both material and immaterial, created by the application of mental and physical effort in order to achieve some value. In this usage, technology refers to tools and machines that may be used to solve real-world problems. It is a far-reaching term that may include simple tools, such as a crowbar or wooden spoon, or more complex machines, such as a space station or particle accelerator. Tools and machines need not be material; virtual technology, such as computer software and business methods, fall under this definition of technology. Brian Arthur defines technology in a similarly broad way as "a means to fulfill a human purpose."

A robot is a machine capable of carrying out a complex series of actions automatically. Robots can be guided by an external control device or the control may be embedded within. Robots may be constructed to take on human form but most robots are machines designed to perform a task with no regard to how they look.

Childhood obesity is a condition where excess body fat negatively affects a child's health or well-being. As methods to determine body fat directly are difficult, the diagnosis of obesity is often based on BMI. Due to the rising prevalence of obesity in children and its many adverse health effects, it is being recognized as a serious public health concern.

Every living cell, tissue and organ of the human body contains water. It's second only to oxygen as the thing we need most to survive. We're constantly in need of water, as we lose it with every breath we take and every activity we do. We're especially at risk of dehydration when we exercise or when we're ill. This is when dehydration can take its toll, causing us to feel lethargic and weak. Young children, the elderly and people with chronic illnesses are most at risk. To prevent dehydration you should mostly drink water rather than fruit juices, soft drinks, alcohol, tea and coffee. You should also avoid exercising during the heat of the day and eat plenty of foods that have a high water content like fruits and vegetables. Ultimately, water should be your main beverage.

It's important to understand where antiques originate. Valuable time is wasted by collectors and dealers looking in places where they think there is an abundance of antiques. But many well meaning dealers and collectors don't seem to have real knowledge of where antiques come from – not originally from auctions, flea markets, or antique shows but in people's homes. This is a basic truth for 99% of all antiques that you will ever see. The good news about antiques is they can be less expensive than reproductions because you're not paying for materials and labor costs at today's prices.

The most important decision when building your own home is the size of your budget. This will determine each step of the building process through to completion and beyond. Remember, of course,  to add the cost of your land to the budget of the entire project. There are plenty of reasons why construction costs can increase slightly so be sure to leave a bit extra in your budget for changes to the plan. The biggest determining factor of your budget is going to be securing financing. Contacting lenders is the next step towards building your home. Make sure to shop around for the best rates and situation that fits your finances best.

Before you get a tattoo, ask the artist to go through the entire procedure with you, including all the tools to be used and the specific design. Everything should be displayed in full view just for your tattoo—for example, ink bottles that are single-use and disposable. The artist should wear rubber gloves and apply Vaseline to the new tattoo with a disposable applicator, not with their bare hands!

Also, think carefully about the design. Will you love it in a year, five years, or ten years? Avoid getting a name that you might want to hide in the future. Children’s and parents’ names are usually safe choices.

When you access the internet through the world wide web it is extremely important that you safe guard your personal information. It is estimated there are several thousand people world wide making very good money exploiting loop holes in security by accessing personal identities, passwords and bank accounts. There are many more daily who are the victims of these crimes. You have a responsibility as a user of the internet to take certain precautions to protect yourself from online crime. Internet users who access bank accounts, e-currency accounts or any source of personal information online should be aware of the various methods used by criminals to convince you to reveal your confidential information.

A good school sports program should require a physical and a medical release form signed by a physician. Prior to the check-up, the doctor should be told the child will be participating in sports so that he can look for and identify preexisting conditions such as asthma and other pulmonary disorders, and any heart conditions. The increased ozone and pollution levels that come with summer heat merit close attention too, as they can exacerbate physical conditions that might otherwise be easily managed. Get the green light from a doctor before letting your child commence a sport.

Green tea offers many health benefits for those who drink it regularly. Besides offering antioxidant benefits, green tea is excellent as a weight loss supplement offering weight loss results and safety. Of course, green tea should be used in combination with a healthy diet and exercise program. Dietary intake of green tea has been linked to a reduced risk of heart disease and cancer. Regular consumption of green tea shows no significant side effects. Green tea helps prevent cavities, strengthens teeth, and is thought to kill oral bacteria that is responsible for bad breath. Green tea has been shown to protect the brain from oxidative stress and to increase exercise endurance by improving the metabolism of fat.

Travel insurance policies are typically designed to cover a wide range of travel needs, but when it comes to deductibles and particular health situations, I urge you to carefully read the fine print. Your policy may not cover preexisting conditions or the medical coverage may only cover injury or illness, not both. In terms of emergency aid, travel insurance policies are mostly reliable and inexpensive, but they can still have lots of gaps in their coverage. There are policies designed for almost all types of trip - like single trips, hunting trips, backpacking, cruises, house rentals and many others.

Few decisions cause as much worry and angst, apart from a credit card, as to whether or not your teenager needs a phone. The cellular phone companies would certainly advocate for allowing your teenager the use of a cell phone. Teenagers can, of course, use their phone for emergencies such as when they break down on the side of the road or when they unexpectedly need a ride home. Don't forget that nearly half of today's teenagers actually already have a cell phone, so leaving your teenager without one could affect their cool factor with their friends and other teenagers.

From flooding and hurricanes to mudslides and blizzards, we all need to be prepared for disasters. First, you should stay educated. Know what the possibility for disaster in your area is. Some areas have fewer disasters than others. But there are always surprises. In the winter, you may live in an area of the country where you should keep your eye on the weather report for snow conditions. Some people must worry about hurricane or tornado season. Learn the basics of food storage and useful crafts. You will be able to get through a disaster if you know how to make things work. You may need to know how to make things from scratch or may do without electricity. Learn the things that will help you survive.

Whether you are learning music from a book, a video or a real teacher, keep these pointers in mind to get the most out of your studies. Before moving on to the next lesson, make sure you have mastered each new technique or idea - as each lesson builds upon previous ones, and trying to rush through without fully understanding one causes frustration and wasted effort. Practicing every day or night, even for just 15 minutes is vital too. Budding musicians must also apply what they have learned by playing for others. Nothing cements a music lesson better than a recital because it magnifies what you need to work on.

To motivate your staff to ensure a safer workplace, think about these tips. Firstly, lead by example. Be genuinely enthusiastic about the safety of your people. Without them, there will be no one to manage or lead. Think safe and live safe, and it will become who you are and how you feel to your peers and employees. Secondly, be supportive of your employees. If you demonstrate to them that their safety comes before production and profit, you will see increases in efficiency and profitability across the board. Above all, show them you care about their safety and well-being and you will reap the benefits.

Renting a car can be a fun way to get around on holiday, but bear in mind driving in unfamiliar places can be challenging, even stressful. Before you book your hire car, make sure driving is the right holiday transport for you. If driving is the right choice for you, remember safety first. Always have insurance so you will have financial back up if an accident occurs. And be sure to inspect the rental car for damage before you take it out, so that you will be aware of any preexisting damage. Also, make sure the gas tank has been filled before you leave the rental depot. Above all else is the safety of you and your passengers, so drive responsibly.

When you look at the rubbish lying beside many roads a lot of it is plastic drink containers. Over 2.2 billion per year are sold now, up from 130 million in 1989. Something must be done. If we cannot control ourselves and continually feel the need to just dump our trash, well then maybe we ought to make it easier to recycle for those who cannot control themselves. Why not color code roadside rubbish bins so drivers can chuck them into the containers paid for by the churches to raise money for recycling? Or pay the homeless or unemployed to pick up the trash to take to recycling depots?

How do builders decide what to charge for a new home? You already know you pay for the timber, the floor coverings and the fixtures, but how is the final price calculated? It's based on many different things, including the construction of the house, the land and the marketing and admin costs for the builder. The construction constitutes about 50% of the base price of the home and there are several costs within the construction factor such as the cost of the materials that go into the home, from the timber to the concrete, doors and windows. And then there are the labor costs associated with work performed by the builder's employees.

A report from Harvard Medical Surveys revealed four of five patients who file medical malpractice claims aren't injured through negligence. And the vast majority of patients who have suffered negligent injury don't sue. Even though injured patients are 20 times more likely to sue than patients who aren't injured, medical error doesn't drive malpractice claims. Most patients sue because of emotional errors. They feel deserted or feel their views were devaluated or they feel their physician failed to understand their perspective. Your goal then as a medical professional is to uncover your patients' needs and negotiate solutions that meet their needs as well as the needs of your hospital.

Skimming is the most basic form of reading. The aim of skim reading is not to grasp everything you read, but to quickly familiarize yourself with the text being read. When you skim the index, headings and sub-headings you ready yourself so that when the time comes you can speed read at pace as you are already familiar with the text. Consequently, your overall reading speed will be much improved. Skimming also allows your subconscious the chance to absorb the main ideas of a text, which is cemented by your speed reading of the contents. Together these two methods allow you greater comprehension of what you are reading.

Some nations are trying to figure out a way to make hurricanes so they can better understand them with a view to lessening their destructive impact. Creating one is going to be far easier than killing one before it develops into a superstorm, that's for sure. Perhaps, though, we should concentrate on both -  killing hurricanes and understanding how to make them so we can use this knowledge to prevent them or steer them away from populated areas to minimize damage to property and life. However, let's not forget once a category 4 or 5 hurricane gets going that is a massive amount of energy to deal with.

Many people are worried about pesticides and their effect on health. In the UK alone over 30,000 tons of pesticides are used each year. While farm workers have direct contact with them we are all affected in some way through pesticide residues in and on food, in the air, from spraying in fields and contamination of drinking water. Various UK health department studies have discovered 43% of all fruit and vegetables, 71% of cereal bars and 83% of oily fish have pesticide residues on them. Unfortunately, we don't yet know enough about pesticides to be certain they're safe for us and only harmful to pests.

Hawaiian Islanders traditionally used surfboards made of hardwood but they were very heavy and awkward to carry which is why lighter boards were later developed using other materials. Man-made materials like fiberglass have made surfboards not just lighter but far easier to turn in the water as well. While the vast majority of today's surfboards are of fiberglass there is a trend in some parts of the surfing world where old-style wooden boards are making a comeback. It's hard to say why but modern fiberglass boards tend to snap in big surf and they can be more expensive to repair than wooden ones.

Sales of SUVs (Sport Utility Vehicles) have rocketed in New Zealand in recent years. In 2016, SUVs made up 13% of new vehicle sales with over 350,000 on the road. However, SUV's are bigger and heavier than other cars meaning buyers can overlook their safety issues. Their higher centre of gravity makes them unstable and prone to rolling over. SUVs are the leading cause of rollover deaths on highways. The latest statistics reveal over 250 people died in the last 2 years in rollover accidents with SUVs accounting for 62 percent of them. When planning for any type of financial situation, first educate yourself. When it comes to retirement planning, many people base their decisions on preconceived notions that are unsupported by reality. There are many things you need to know when preparing for retirement. You don't need the same annual income in retirement that you had when you were working. Most retirees only need 85% of their pre-retirement income to live a comfortable life. This isn't set in stone, though, as retirement is an individual matter. Therefore, look closely at your particular lifestyle when determining how much you will need to get through your twilight years.

It's crucial when storing wine to avoid large temperature fluctuations. A tell tale sign of this occurring is when you spot a sticky deposit forming around the capsule. Over time the cork becomes damaged due to the continual expansion and contraction of the wine, whereby very tiny amounts of wine are pushed out between the cork and the bottle neck allowing air to seep back in. Once your wine and the air make contact, oxidation begins and this is what ruins your wine. It is an irreversible process so if you're storing expensive wines you must store them properly.

Over 1.2 million skin cancer cases are diagnosed each year and tens of thousands of people die from it each year. Skin cancer cases actually exceed that of all other cancers combined. Melanoma is the deadliest skin cancer and there is no cure for it. While children are at risk of getting skin cancer as they spend more time outdoors than adults, it typically doesn't become apparent until adulthood after many years of sun exposure. Bear in mind that the ultraviolet rays that cause skin cancer are not just present on hot, summer days. Regardless of the season or temperature ultraviolet rays are still there.

The first trick to getting your windows squeaky clean is to invest in a good squeegee. The cheap $2.00 ones will not do. A good quality squeegee has a soft, rubber blade with no imperfections in it and it comes with extra rubber blades, all for about $20-30. The second trick is to do your windows only on cool or cloudy days. Hot days are no good as heat works against you, making it difficult to get beautifully clean windows. The final trick is to add about 3 tablespoons of ammonia to a bucket of lukewarm water. This is what makes the glass so clean it squeaks when you touch it.

Pepper spray is one of the best methods of self-defense for individuals and law enforcement officers. It can quickly eliminate threats without causing long term damage. It is strong enough to give users enough time to get away and police officers enough time to apprehend offenders or defuse a tense situation. Pepper spray is derived from cayenne or black pepper, which contain many potent organic compounds that impair vision and respiration when sprayed into the face. Subjects tend to feel the acute effects of pepper spray for several minutes, giving you time to flee as their attention shifts to their burning eyes and throat.

To find out what the right toys are for your child just observe them as they go about their life. You'll soon know what their preferred activities and pastimes are, what their skill levels are and so on. Your keen observations as a parent will soon give you clues about the sorts of toys your child would prefer. Your child just watched and loved Lord of the Rings? Then chances are he'll also like Lord of the Rings toys. It won't always be this simple, though, and your observations won't always be enough. Your child's age, gender and his or her playmates will also help steer you in the right direction.

Tsunamis are often wrongly called tidal waves. The difference is - a tidal wave is a term used for a shallow water wave caused by the interaction between gravitational forces of the sun, the moon and the earth. Tsunamis, on the other hand, form when water is displaced. This can occur as a result of a volcanic eruption, an underwater earthquake and sometimes a landslide if it is large enough. A tsunami might only be 1 meter high out in the open ocean, but as it nears the shoreline and shallower water it can suddenly increase to 30 meters or more and move at 400 to 500 miles per hour.

Wayne was born in 1907 and his real name was Marion Robert Morrison. John Wayne starred in dozens of movies like Lawless Range, Ringo Kid and The Lucky Texan. In 1969 he won an Academy Award for his role in True Grit. The Shootest in 1976 was the Duke's final movie.

In his great novel Remembrance of Things Past or In Search of Lost Time, Marcel Proust explored what are called “involuntary memories” – those that come to us quite suddenly without conscious effort, usually triggered by one of the senses. The fact that his book has two titles in English might suggest there is some doubt as to how our minds go about remembering things. In perhaps the most well- known such episode in the novel, the character Marcel is reminded of his childhood by the aroma produced when dunking a cake in a cup of tea. This is not far away from the belief that as we get older we can remember quite clearly incidents from years ago, but find it hard to remember what we did last week. Proust’s insight into memory is certainly true of one way the mind works. But why and how do we remember what we do?

Experts believe that we store memories in three ways. First, there is the sensory stage which is to do with perception and lasts only a fraction of a second, taking in sight, sound, touch, and so on. These first perceptions and sensations are then stored in the short-term memory, which is the second stage. Finally, important information or information that has been reinforced by, for example, repetition, is then filtered into the long-term memory. Naturally, we tend to more easily absorb material on things we already know something about, as this has more meaning for us and can create a web of connections with related material that is already stored in the long-term memory.

I want to look now at the three main approaches historians have taken towards the English Revolution or Civil War. There is a fourth point of view, taken up by most schoolchildren when they first meet the subject, but it can hardly be called historical. On the one hand, schoolchildren tend to have a romantic image of the Cavaliers, who were supporters of the monarchy, as aristocratic, charming, flashily dressed, and up for a bit of fun. On the other hand, they tend to regard the Roundheads or Puritans, who were followers of Oliver Cromwell, as miserable, working-class, dressed in black, and insisting on a life without luxuries. It isn’t difficult then to takes sides.

The first approach, which prevailed up until the middle of the 20th century, was that the Revolution was part of the age-old battle between parliament and the monarchy, with parliament representing the traditional rights of Englishmen against the attempt by the Royal Family to increase its power and dictate law. In reaction to this, the second approach saw it as a working-class revolution and an important stage in the development of capitalism. In other words, they saw it as a class war, and a forerunner of the French Revolution and those that came after. Historians who supported the third approach saw that things weren’t as clear cut as the others thought. Instead of seeing the Revolution as the result of long-term trends in the country’s history, and therefore almost unavoidable, they focused on the details of the period immediately leading up to its outbreak and allowed for its unpredictability. The two sides, also, weren’t so clear cut, with some aristocrats supporting parliament and some members of the working class fighting on the side of the monarchy.

How did I get into anthropology? Well, I’m not an anthropologist, not trained in it anyway - in fact I’m a marine biologist - and it’s more that I am interested in the Strandlopers, the “beach walkers”, if you like, than in anthropology generally. In fact, it was through my early interest in all things to do with the sea that I first heard of them. As a kid my favorite pastime was getting down to the beach and mucking about in rock pools and collecting anything the sea tossed up, and, um, the Strandlopers lived off the sea and seashore gathering food such as mussels, oysters, crabs, and so on, just as I did. So when I heard of them I thought, right, this is my kind of thing and I felt a kind of, well, affinity for them.

At first people thought they were a myth; I suppose because there didn’t seem to be much evidence for their existence, but now we do have archaeological evidence such as pottery, discarded shells, the bones of seals and large fish, and so on. And those shells, we believe, became their first tools, and in some cases were also used as money and jewellery. Of course, before they had the implements, the, um, tools to hunt and fish, everything had to be caught by hand, so they kept mostly to shallow water. Then they might have used a primitive form of fishing line, perhaps the tendril of a climbing plant or a strip of animal skin with a bone or small piece of wood that would stick in the fish’s throat. Anyway, the more we search the more we discover - most recently, caves on the coast have been discovered with ancient drawings. I find it endlessly fascinating.

To reach some kind of understanding of a period in the past - the Middle Ages, say - requires a creative act of the imagination not unlike that of a novelist getting inside the skins of their characters or establishing the environment they live in. What must it have been like to be a peasant under a feudal baron? What might his beliefs and politics have been? Did he, for example, think his situation was simply part of the natural order of things, or that it was grossly unfair and needed to be overthrown?

So, you have to imagine the terms under which life was lived in those days, and learn to feel in a different way about things by putting yourself into a world very different from your own. Now, of course, every age views the past from its own present and an 18th-century historian’s concept of the Middle Ages will be very different from that of one from the 21st century - which is why each age has to write its history over again. It’s not so much that more facts or evidence come to light - if they do - but that sensibilities change too.

I want to take a quick look at how material culture, that is, the things or objects we value and use, tools, technology, etc., affects the non- material culture, our customs, behavior, beliefs, attitudes, and so on. Now, of course, it works both ways: if you were, say, eighteen years old when mobile phones became widespread or when almost every home had a computer, you must have noticed a fairly significant change in people’s attitudes and behavior, including your own. These two technological innovations were readily accepted and considered very much a positive contribution to culture.

On the other hand, there is the way in which our attitudes and beliefs have an effect on the material culture. Take, for example, genetic science. While many, if not most, people welcome the advances made in this field - from the discovery of DNA to the genome project and cracking the genetic code, with its promise of finding cures for diseases that have plagued mankind throughout history - many, too, object to the idea of cloning. This is not just because they fear, without reason, that it will mean creating a race of identical people if it gets into the wrong hands, but simply because it is, in their opinion, unnatural. It goes against everything they believe in. Similarly, there are many who object to genetically modified food, even when they are told it might be the only way to feed a rapidly growing global population.

Ernest Hemingway was famously masculine. We can see this in the topics he wrote about: all those fights and bullfights, all those wars, all that hunting and fishing. All this, it is thought, was overdoing it and must point to some deep insecurity in the man. Perhaps he felt that the conventional image of a writer as a person who doesn’t do a lot more than sitting at a desk, not visibly contributing much socially or economically to society as a whole was unmanly and carried a certain amount of guilt and shame with it?

During the First World War, he was badly wounded, hospitalized, and fell in love with a nurse. He wrote a novel about a man who was badly wounded in the First World War, was hospitalised and who fell in love with a nurse. The Spanish Civil War and the Second World War followed as did books about his experiences in them. Now, how much can we draw on a writer’s biography to explain his work? It should, I believe, be irrelevant to the judgement you bring to the merits of the individual work.

Biographies of writers can be fascinating, especially if they lived as hard and fast as Hemingway, but, unfortunately, these days many people prefer the biography to the actual work. It is odd that the very reason a person would want to read a biography of a writer - that the subject has achieved fame and distinction in his field - should be the excuse for not reading his works.

This is the first of the Houston film lectures, a series of lectures from the National University of Ireland Houston school of film and digital media in Galway. The lecture series features leading film directors, writers, producers, cinematographers and academics. In this lecture, Irish feminist filmmaker Pat Murphy looks at what are the influences on her as a filmmaker. The people, the events, the ideas and the context that has influenced her work. Pat Murphy grew up in Northern Ireland and studied in London at Hornsey College of Art and the Royal College of Art in the 1970s. Her films "Maeve" and "Devlin" and in 2001,"Nora" starring Susan Lynch and Ewan McGregor have made her one of Ireland's most important filmmakers.

The question: Why do students achieve so much with one teacher and so little under the guidance of another teacher? is often asked. I believe as a result of having done research in this area for some time, developing good classroom management is a determining factor respect is paramount. Students need to feel respected and in turn, need to develop respect for the teacher. I've spoken to kids who delight in moking up in class and I've talked to their teachers. A common nonverbal cue they both tend to give is rolling their eyes. Oh that class, the teacher recoils, oh him, the student groans. What's clear here is a lack of respect and don't start to think as a teacher you'll be able to fake it students are quick to smell a rat. Teachers who are not able to respect their students are in the wrong game.
